skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents using inference.sh. It opens pages, returns compact element refs, interacts with web controls, captures screenshots, records videos, runs page JavaScript, supports proxies, and includes shell workflow templates.
skill.summary: Automate browser sessions with element refs, screenshots, video, JavaScript, proxy support, and reusable shell workflows.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and sensitive-data patterns. Most shell detections are documented inference.sh examples, but the skill also enables authenticated browsing, cookie extraction, file upload, screenshots, video recording, arbitrary page JavaScript, proxy rotation, and a pipe-to-shell installer. No prompt injection attempt was found, and no confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Authenticated Session and Cookie Extraction Guidance
high_findings.description: The authentication guide demonstrates filling passwords, persisting authenticated sessions, extracting document cookies, and printing cookies. This can expose session tokens if used on accounts without strict consent and redaction.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly automate credential entry and retrieve cookies from an authenticated browser session. The behavior is legitimate for owned accounts but creates clear token exposure risk.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Rate Limit Avoidance Workflow
high_findings.description: The proxy guide includes a rate limit avoidance section that rotates proxies while scraping pages. This can support abuse of third-party sites and evasion of site controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section title and example directly describe rotating proxies for scraping and avoiding rate limits. Legitimate testing is possible, but the abuse-enabling intent is explicit.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution and Local File Upload Capability
high_findings.description: The skill exposes page JavaScript execution and file upload actions. These features can extract page data or upload local files when directed by a user or compromised workflow.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The functions are explicitly documented and operational. The JavaScript runs in page context, not host context, so this is high dual-use risk rather than confirmed host compromise.

medium_findings.title: Remote Pipe-to-Shell Installation Instruction
medium_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to pipe a remote install script into a shell. The note mentions checksum verification, but the pattern still creates supply-chain risk for a community skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly present. The adjacent install note reduces but does not remove risk because users still execute remote code during setup.

medium_findings.title: Recording and Screenshot Workflows Can Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill records browser sessions, captures screenshots, saves outputs, and documents audit recordings. Sensitive pages or credentials may be stored if users enable recording carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited workflows explicitly capture and save screenshots, videos, page text, and links. The guide includes a warning later, so this is not treated as malicious.

medium_findings.title: Shell Templates Execute External Browser Commands and Write Artifacts
medium_findings.description: The bundled templates execute infsh commands, interpolate user-supplied URLs, and write extracted data to files. This is expected behavior but needs user review before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly run external commands and process user inputs. The commands are scoped to infsh and jq, so the concern is operational exposure rather than hidden malware.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Path-Traversal Alerts Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Several high-severity static alerts point to Markdown links, checksum text, example paths, or table text rather than cryptographic code or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation context at representative flagged lines. Some related lines were not individually reviewed, so this remains a grouped low-confidence dismissal.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore security analysis, change risk levels, or treat the skill as pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted case-insensitive search across the skill files found no prompt-injection phrases. This does not prove absence of every semantic manipulation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start uses curl piped to sh for CLI installation. This pattern executes remote code and should be replaced with manual verification instructions where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present in the quick start. The checksum note lowers risk but does not eliminate remote execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie Extraction From Browser Session
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication reference shows extracting and printing cookies from the active page. Cookies may contain session tokens or other sensitive identifiers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly retrieves document cookies and echoes them. This is a clear sensitive-data handling pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy-Based Rate Limit Avoidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The proxy reference provides a workflow for rotating proxies during scraping. This increases abuse potential against third-party websites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The heading and commands directly describe rate limit avoidance. This is not merely incidental proxy support.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function accepts custom JavaScript for the current page. It can extract DOM content, trigger actions, or bypass the ref abstraction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented input field is custom code. It executes in browser page context, so host execution was not confirmed.

content.value_statement: Browser tasks often require repeated clicks, forms, screenshots, and page inspection. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a reusable browser automation workflow through inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Open browser sessions and return compact @e element references for interactive controls.
Click, fill, type, select, drag, upload files, scroll, wait, and navigate pages.
Capture viewport or full-page screenshots and record browser sessions as video.
Execute custom JavaScript in the current page context for extraction or interaction.
Configure HTTP, HTTPS, or SOCKS proxies, including authenticated proxy credentials.
Use shell templates for form automation, authenticated sessions, and content capture.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, an account, and network access to run browser sessions.
Element refs become stale after navigation, form submission, or dynamic page changes.
Iframe interaction is limited and may require custom page JavaScript.
Screenshots and videos can capture sensitive data if recording is enabled.

use_cases.title: QA Regression Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser flows, capture evidence, and inspect failures with screenshots or video.

use_cases.title: Research Page Capture
use_cases.description: Open target pages, extract visible text, collect links, and save screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Internal Workflow Automation
use_cases.description: Automate approved forms and authenticated tasks when credentials and data handling are controlled.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target URL with agent-browser. Show the page title, current URL, screenshot status, and available @e refs.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Simple Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect this form, fill the required fields with my provided values, submit it, and verify the result.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Research Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Browse these approved pages, capture screenshots, extract visible text and links, and summarize key findings with source URLs.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Authenticated Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use an existing approved session to reproduce this workflow, record only non-sensitive steps, and report failed refs or page changes.

output_examples.input: Inspect a product page and capture useful evidence.
output_examples.output: A concise page report with title, URL, visible controls, extracted text highlights, link summary, and screenshot availability.

output_examples.input: Submit a test contact form and verify completion.
output_examples.output: A workflow summary showing filled fields, submitted action, final page state, success indicators, and any validation messages.

output_examples.input: Record a failing browser interaction for review.
output_examples.output: A debugging note with the failed action, current page state, likely cause, and the saved recording reference.

best_practices: Use only approved websites, accounts, and data sources before automating browser actions.
Disable video recording when pages may show passwords, tokens, payment data, or private records.
Re-snapshot after every navigation, form submission, modal change, or dynamic content update.
anti_patterns: Do not use proxy rotation to evade rate limits, access controls, or website terms.
Do not print, store, or share cookies, passwords, screenshots, or videos containing secrets.
Do not run bundled shell templates before reviewing URLs, output paths, and credential handling.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, video recording, JavaScript execution, proxy setup, and common shell workflows.

faq.question: Does it require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It relies on inference.sh and the infsh CLI to run the browser automation functions.

faq.question: Can it handle login flows?
faq.answer: Yes, but only use approved accounts and avoid logging passwords, cookies, screenshots, or videos with sensitive data.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The execute function runs custom JavaScript in the current page context, which can extract or modify page data.

faq.question: Why are @e refs important?
faq.answer: They provide compact references for clickable and editable elements, but they must be refreshed after page changes.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish without review?
faq.answer: No. It is a high-risk dual-use browser automation skill and should receive human review before publication.
